AWS Config provides a number of AWS-managed rules that address a wide range of security concerns, such as checking if you encrypted your Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volumes, tagged your resources appropriately, and enabled multi-factor authentication (MFA) for root accounts.

AWS Config and How it works

Hence, the correct answer is: Use AWS Config.

The options that say: Configure the bucket policy to deny all read and write actions from unauthorized users and Configure the bucket policy of the S3 bucket using IAM to prevent any unauthorized access are incorrect, as simply configuring the S3 bucket policy is not enough to satisfy the given requirements. It does not provide a way to automate the assessment of your resource configurations and resource changes, unlike AWS Config.

The option that says: Enable MFA Delete and Versioning in the S3 bucket is incorrect because versioning and MFA delete is mainly used to prevent accidental deletion of objects in the bucket. These features do not automate the assessment of your resource configurations and resource changes.

questions.question:
A leading advertising company is using Lambda to run a serverless application in AWS. The application processes transactions on an Amazon RDS database instance with MySQL support. As the company grows, the number of users has started to increase. As a result, your Lambda function is beginning to receive “too many connections” errors when attempting to connect to the RDS database. When the CloudOps Engineer checked the database settings, the max_connections parameter is already set to the maximum possible value.

What step should the CloudOps Engineer perform to resolve the issue?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you resolve "too many connections" errors when a Lambda function connects to an RDS database at maximum capacity?
choices: Raise the Lambda function's provisioned concurrency setting to a higher value.
Increase the value of the max_connect_errors parameter to 10 to allow more errors.
Provision an Amazon RDS Proxy and update the database connection string in your Lambda function.
Enable Multi-AZ setting for the RDS database instance.
correctAnswers: Provision an Amazon RDS Proxy and update the database connection string in your Lambda function.
questions.explanation:
Amazon RDS Proxy is a fully managed, highly available database proxy for Amazon Relational Database Service (RDS) that makes applications more scalable, more resilient to database failures, and more secure.

Many applications, including those built on modern serverless architectures, can have a large number of open connections to the database server, and may open and close database connections at a high rate, exhausting database memory and compute resources. Amazon RDS Proxy allows applications to pool and share connections established with the database, improving database efficiency and application scalability.

It is stated in the scenario that there are too many connections in the database due to an increase in user traffic. This means your RDS instance just needs to accommodate more database connections. But since the database in the scenario is already set to use the maximum number of connections, we can utilize Amazon RDS proxy to increase the number of DB connections that the infrastructure can handle. To do this, you just need to create a proxy server and replace the connection string on your application.

Hence, the correct answer is: Provision an Amazon RDS Proxy and update the database connection string in your Lambda function.

The option that says: Increase the value of the max_connect_errors parameter to 10 is incorrect because this just determines the maximum number of attempts a host can try before further connections get blocked.

The option that says: Enable Multi-AZ setting for the RDS database instance is incorrect because this just creates a failover instance that is on standby and waits for failover to happen. It will not improve the performance of the database to resolve the increased number of database connections.

The option that says: Raise the Lambda function’s provisioned concurrency setting to a higher value is incorrect because this just initializes a requested number of execution environments so that AWS is prepared to respond immediately to your function’s invocations.

questions.question:
The company’s AWS billing report is currently generated on a monthly basis. To strengthen financial monitoring, the accounting department instructed the CloudOps Engineer to implement a mechanism for receiving billing updates more frequently than once a month. A spreadsheet application will be used to view and analyze the billing data.

Which of the following is the MOST suitable solution that the Engineer should implement for this scenario?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What is the best way to generate daily AWS billing reports in CSV format for spreadsheet analysis?
choices: Configure the AWS Cost and Usage Report to generate and publish billing reports in CSV format to an Amazon S3 bucket every day.
Use a combination of AWS Lambda and Amazon EventBridge to generate billing reports every week. Enable report data integration for Amazon QuickSight to convert the data output to a CSV format.
Configure the AWS Budgets to generate and publish billing reports in CSV format to an Amazon S3 bucket every day.
Use a combination of AWS Lambda and Amazon CloudWatch Alarms to generate billing reports by querying the AWS Cost Explorer API every week. Utilize AWS Glue to convert the data output to a CSV format.
correctAnswers: Configure the AWS Cost and Usage Report to generate and publish billing reports in CSV format to an Amazon S3 bucket every day.
questions.explanation:
In AWS Cost and Usage reports, you can choose to have AWS publish billing reports to an Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) bucket that you own. You can receive reports that break down your costs by the hour or month, by product or product resource, or by tags that you define yourself. AWS updates the report in your bucket once a day in a comma-separated value (CSV) format. You can view the reports using spreadsheet software such as Microsoft Excel or Apache OpenOffice Calc or access them from an application using the Amazon S3 API.

Cost and Usage Report

You can configure your Cost & Usage Reports to integrate with Amazon Athena. Once Amazon Athena integration has been enabled for your Cost & Usage Report, your data will be delivered in compressed Apache Parquet files to an Amazon S3 bucket of your choice. Your AWS Cost & Usage Report can also be ingested directly into Amazon Redshift or uploaded to Amazon QuickSight.

If you use the consolidated billing feature in AWS Organizations, the Amazon S3 bucket that you designate to receive the billing reports must be owned by the master account in your organization. You can’t receive billing reports in a bucket that is owned by a member account. If you use consolidated billing, you can also have your costs broken down by member account.

In this scenario, you can configure your AWS Cost and Usage Report to generate and publish billing reports in CSV format to an S3 bucket every day. The CSV output can be ingested into the spreadsheet application of the accounting department for viewing and analysis.

Hence, the correct answer is: Configure the AWS Cost and Usage Report to generate and publish billing reports in CSV format to an Amazon S3 bucket every day.

The option that says: Use a combination of AWS Lambda and Amazon CloudWatch Alarms to generate billing reports by querying the AWS Cost Explorer API every week. Utilize AWS Glue to convert the data output to a CSV format is incorrect. Although it is possible to meet the requirement by querying the AWS Cost Explorer API and using AWS Glue, this solution entails a lot of unnecessary work that can be solved by simply switching to AWS Cost and Usage Report. Moreover, you have to use a combination of AWS Lambda and Amazon EventBridge instead of CloudWatch Alarms to make a scheduled job of fetching data from the AWS Cost Explorer API every week.

The option that says: Configure the AWS Budgets to generate and publish billing reports in CSV format to an Amazon S3 bucket every day is incorrect because AWS Budgets only gives you the ability to set custom budgets that alert you when your costs or usage exceed. You should use the AWS Cost and Usage Report instead.

The option that says: Use a combination of AWS Lambda and Amazon EventBridge to generate billing reports every week. Enable report data integration for Amazon QuickSight to convert the data output to a CSV format is incorrect because Amazon QuickSight is a fully managed service that lets you easily create and publish interactive dashboards. This is not used to convert your data to a CSV format. You also don’t need to use either AWS Lambda or Amazon EventBridge in this scenario, as this can be solved by using AWS Cost and Usage Report.

questions.question:
A law firm uses an Amazon RDS DB instance to store data on their online inquiries and consultations. The firm is managing several Lambda functions that create, update, and delete records from the database. The DB credentials are hardcoded on the Lambda functions, which are used to connect to the database.

The CloudOps Engineer wants to improve the setup by ensuring that the DB credentials are not stored in plaintext and that the DB password is constantly rotated every 30 days.

What steps should the CloudOps Engineer perform to achieve the requirements in the MOST operationally efficient way?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What is the most operationally efficient way to store and automatically rotate RDS credentials for Lambda?
choices: Encrypt the DB password using the AWS Key Management Service (KMS). Store the encrypted password as an environment variable in all Lambda functions. Then, grant each function the necessary permissions to access the KMS key so that the database password can be decrypted when needed. Build a Lambda function that can programmatically reset the DB password every 30 days.

Use AWS Secrets Manager secrets instead of environment variables to store database credentials. While creating the secret, select the database so that the Secrets Manager will use a Lambda function to rotate the password for the database automatically. Specify the automatic rotation schedule to 30 days. Update all the Lambda functions to access the DB password from Secrets Manager.

Create an environment variable in each of the Lambda functions and store the DB password. Then, create a new Lambda function and name it PasswordRotate. Schedule the new function to run every 30 days using Amazon EventBridge (Amazon CloudWatch) to generate a new password and update the environment variable on each Lambda function.

Utilize AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to create secure strings for DB credentials. Build a new Lambda function and name it PasswordRotate. Schedule the new function to run every 30 days using Amazon EventBridge (Amazon CloudWatch) to generate a new password. Modify the environment variable on each Lambda function to access the secure strings stored in Parameter Store.
correctAnswers: Use AWS Secrets Manager secrets instead of environment variables to store database credentials. While creating the secret, select the database so that the Secrets Manager will use a Lambda function to rotate the password for the database automatically. Specify the automatic rotation schedule to 30 days. Update all the Lambda functions to access the DB password from Secrets Manager.
questions.explanation:
You can use environment variables to adjust your function’s behavior without updating code. An environment variable is a pair of strings that are stored in a function’s version-specific configuration. The Lambda runtime makes environment variables available to your code and sets additional environment variables that contain information about the function and invocation request.

To increase database security, we recommend that you use AWS Secrets Manager instead of environment variables to store database credentials.

This approach will help you secure last-mile secrets and protect your backend databases. Long living credentials need to be managed and regularly rotated to keep access into critical systems secure, so it’s a security best practice to periodically reset your passwords. Manually changing the passwords would be cumbersome, but AWS Secrets Manager helps by managing and rotating the RDS database passwords.

Hence, the correct answer is: Use AWS Secrets Manager secrets instead of environment variables to store database credentials. While creating the secret, select the database so that the Secrets Manager will use a Lambda function to rotate the password for the database automatically. Specify the automatic rotation schedule to 30 days. Modify all the Lambda functions to access the DB password from Secrets Manager.

The option that says: Create an environment variable in each of the Lambda functions and store the DB password. Then, create a new Lambda function and name it PasswordRotate. Schedule the new function to run every 30 days using Amazon EventBridge (Amazon CloudWatch) to generate a new password and update the environment variable on each Lambda function is incorrect. Using environment variables to store database passwords is bad practice. The better approach for storing database credentials like passwords is by storing the credentials using AWS Secret Manager.

The option that says: Encrypt the DB password using the AWS Key Management Service (KMS). Store the encrypted password as an environment variable in all Lambda functions. Then, grant each function the necessary permissions to access the KMS key so that the database password can be decrypted when needed. Build a Lambda function that can programmatically reset the DB password every 30 days is incorrect. The AWS KMS service is only used to store cryptographic keys that are used to encrypt and decrypt data across your AWS workloads. It does not help you store database passwords, but it can be used to encrypt and decrypt secrets.

The option that says: Utilize AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to create secure strings for DB credentials. Build a new Lambda function and name it PasswordRotate. Schedule the new function to run every 30 days using Amazon EventBridge (Amazon CloudWatch) to generate a new password. Modify the environment variable on each Lambda function to access the secure strings stored in Parameter Store is incorrect because this will take time to set up. While this is doable, it is stated in the scenario that you need to find the most operationally efficient method to achieve the requirement. The AWS Secrets Manager automatically handles the rotation of DB passwords, which means you don’t need to create a new Lambda function just for the purpose of rotating passwords.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer launched a bastion host in Amazon VPC to only allow one IP address (143.20.2.137) to establish SSH and RDP connections to the EC2 instances that are deployed to a private subnet. The EC2 instances have an attached security group, with a Group ID of sg-tut5d0jo. To fortify the bastion host, the Engineer added the following inbound rules to its security group:

Type: All TCP Protocol: TCP Port Range: 22 Source: 143.20.2.137/32 Type: All TCP Protocol: TCP Port Range: 3389 Source: 143.20.2.137/32 Type: All TCP Protocol: TCP Port Range: 22 Source: sg-tut5d0jo

However, after checking the VPC Flow Logs, the Engineer discovered that there are IP addresses other than the specified IP address that can access the bastion host. Which of the following is the root cause of this issue?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Why can additional IP addresses access a bastion host when a security group is referenced as a source?
choices: A netmask of /32 allows all IP addresses in the 143.20.2.0 network, other than your IP (143.20.2.137)
The port 3389 provides unrestrictive access to 143.20.2.137 and to other IP addresses
The Network ACL allows access to all IP addresses which overrides the Security Group inbound rules
The allowed IP addresses in the security group of the EC2 instances sg-tut5d0jo are also able to connect to the bastion host
correctAnswers: The allowed IP addresses in the security group of the EC2 instances sg-tut5d0jo are also able to connect to the bastion host
questions.explanation:
In this scenario, the security group of the bastion host has an inbound rule to allow the traffic that comes from the sg-tut5d0jo security group of the EC2 instances. This means that all traffic allowed by that security group can communicate with the bastion host.

A security group acts as a virtual firewall for your instance to control inbound and outbound traffic. When you launch an instance in a VPC, you can assign up to five security groups to the instance. Security groups act at the instance level, not the subnet level. Therefore, each instance in a subnet in your VPC could be assigned to a different set of security groups. If you don’t specify a particular group at launch time, the instance is automatically assigned to the default security group for the VPC.

Hence, the correct answer is: The allowed IP addresses in the security group of the EC2 instances sg-tut5d0jo are also able to connect to the bastion host.

The option that says: A netmask of /32 allows all IP addresses in the 143.20.2.0 network, other than your IP (143.20.2.137) is incorrect because a /32 netmask is limited to one IP address and not the entire IP block.

The option that says: The port 3389 provides unrestrictive access to 143.20.2.137 and to other IP addresses is incorrect because port 3389 is just the port used by Microsoft RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol).

The option that says: The Network ACL allows access to all IP addresses which overrides the Security Group inbound rules is incorrect. Even though a Network ACL can allow or deny all IP addresses from accessing the bastion host, it does not override the rules set in the security group.

questions.question:
A school is planning on recreating their own website by adding new features to it and making it more interactive for visitors. Because of this, they would like to create subdomains that redirects to the new webpages, while reusing their old parent domain registered in an external DNS service for the main page of the website.

What would be a cost-effective solution for creating subdomains without having to migrate the parent domain?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How can you create Route 53 subdomains without migrating the parent domain from an external DNS?
choices: Create a Route 53 hosted zone for the subdomain. Add records for the new subdomain to your Route 53 hosted zone. Update the DNS service for the parent domain by adding name server records for the subdomain.
Create a new subdomain in Route 53 by registering a domain and transferring the server records to their current DNS service.
There is no way to do this in AWS.

Provision EC2 servers with elastic IPs attached to them, and use those to host the new webpages. Then use Route 53 A records to point to the elastic IPs, and create NS records to direct subdomain queries.
correctAnswers: Create a Route 53 hosted zone for the subdomain. Add records for the new subdomain to your Route 53 hosted zone. Update the DNS service for the parent domain by adding name server records for the subdomain.
questions.explanation:
Amazon Route 53 provides highly available and scalable Domain Name System (DNS), domain name registration, and health-checking web services. It is designed to give developers and businesses an extremely reliable and cost effective way to route end users to Internet applications by translating names like example.com into the numeric IP addresses, such as 192.0.2.1, that computers use to connect to each other.

AWS allows the creation of a subdomain that uses Route 53 as the DNS service without having to migrate the parent domain for a cheap cost. The steps for performing this can be found in the reference. You don’t need to register a new domain in Route 53 just for the purpose of creating the subdomains.

Hence, the correct answer is: Create a Route 53 hosted zone for the subdomain. Add records for the new subdomain to your Route 53 hosted zone. Update the DNS service for the parent domain by adding name server records for the subdomain.

The option that says: Create a new subdomain in Route 53 by registering a domain and transferring the server records to their current DNS service is incorrect because this is not cost-effective since it requires registering a new domain and transferring server records

The option that says: Provision EC2 servers with elastic IPs attached to them, and use them to host the new webpages. Then use Route 53 A records to point to the elastic IPs, and create NS records to direct subdomain queries is incorrect because this is too tedious to do. The school only needs a host for their subdomains and there are more efficient ways to solve the problem.

The option that says: There is no way to do this in AWS is incorrect since it is possible to create subdomains in AWS using Route 53.

questions.question:
An application hosted in an On-Demand EC2 instance was launched in a private subnet in your VPC with an IPv4 CIDR block. It was working well and can fetch data from the Internet until it suddenly stopped working. Upon checking your route table, you noticed that the route to 0.0.0.0/0 has a status of blackhole as shown below:

Which of the following is most likely the cause of this issue?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What causes a route table entry to show a blackhole status for 0.0.0.0/0 in a private subnet?
choices: There currently is an intermittent issue in your AWS Account.
The Egress-Only Internet Gateway that was previously attached was deleted.
The Internet Gateway was detached from your VPC.
The NAT Instance or NAT Gateway that was previously attached was deleted.
correctAnswers: The NAT Instance or NAT Gateway that was previously attached was deleted.
questions.explanation:
You can use a network address translation (NAT) gateway to enable instances in a private subnet to connect to the Internet or other AWS services but prevent the Internet from initiating a connection with those instances. NAT gateways are not supported for IPv6 traffic, but you can use an egress-only Internet gateway instead.

To ensure that your NAT gateway can access the internet, the route table associated with the subnet in which your NAT gateway resides must include a route that points Internet traffic to an Internet gateway.

If you delete a NAT gateway, the NAT gateway routes remain in a blackhole status until you delete or update the routes. Hence, the correct answer in this scenario is that the NAT Instance or NAT Gateway that was previously attached was deleted.

The option that says: The Internet Gateway was detached from your VPC is incorrect. Although detaching the Internet Gateway will affect the Internet connectivity of your application, this is unlikely to cause the blackhole status in your route table.

The option that says: The Egress-Only Internet Gateway that was previously attached was deleted is incorrect because an Egress-Only Internet Gateway is primarily used in VPCs with an IPv6 CIDR block. The scenario clearly mentioned the VPC has an IPv4 CIDR block hence, it is more realistic to assume that they are using a NAT Instance or a NAT Gateway.

The option that says: There currently is an intermittent issue in your AWS Account is incorrect because an intermittent issue in AWS won’t cause this problem. Based on the documentation above, this blackhole status will only show up if you delete a NAT gateway and you didn’t update your route table.

questions.question:
A global technology company has a cloud architecture that uses various VPCs across multiple regions. To effectively monitor the entire system, the CloudOps Engineer has been tasked with aggregating the CPU utilization data for all Reserved Amazon EC2 instances operating within these VPCs.

How can the CloudOps Engineer implement this requirement in the easiest way possible with minimal additional costs? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you aggregate CPU utilization metrics across EC2 instances in multiple AWS regions using CloudWatch?
choices: Set up an Amazon CloudWatch dashboard. Add a widget and select Cross-Region under Graph Metric to aggregate the CPU Utilization of all Reserved EC2 instances in all regions.
Enable basic monitoring for all EC2 instances.
Set up an Amazon CloudWatch dashboard. Add a widget and choose Math expression under Graph Metric to query and aggregate the CPU Utilization of all Reserved EC2 instances in all regions.
Enable detailed monitoring for all EC2 instances.
Set up a data dashboard in Amazon QuickSight. Add a widget for each region that contains the aggregated CPU utilization for all EC2 instances that are running in a specific region.
correctAnswers: Set up an Amazon CloudWatch dashboard. Add a widget and choose Math expression under Graph Metric to query and aggregate the CPU Utilization of all Reserved EC2 instances in all regions.
Enable detailed monitoring for all EC2 instances.
questions.explanation:
You can aggregate the metrics for AWS resources across multiple accounts and Regions. For example, you can aggregate statistics for your EC2 instances that have detailed monitoring enabled. Instances that use basic monitoring are not included. Therefore, you must enable detailed monitoring (at an additional charge), which provides data in 1-minute periods.

Amazon EC2 - Detailed monitoring

AWS allows you to use CloudWatch metric math to aggregate and transform metrics from multiple accounts and Regions. Metric math enables you to query multiple CloudWatch metrics and use math expressions to create new time series based on these metrics. You can visualize the resulting time series on the CloudWatch console and add them to dashboards. You can add a math expression to a graph on your CloudWatch dashboard.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Set up an Amazon CloudWatch dashboard. Add a widget and choose Math expression under Graph Metric to query and aggregate the CPU Utilization of all Reserved EC2 instances in all regions.

– Enable detailed monitoring for all EC2 instances.

The option that says: Set up an Amazon CloudWatch dashboard. Add a widget and select Cross-Region under Graph Metric to aggregate the CPU Utilization of all Reserved EC2 instances in all regions is incorrect because there is no option to select Cross-Region under Graph Metric. Although cross-region graphing is supported, you need to use Metric math to aggregate cross-region metrics.

The option that says: Set up a data dashboard in Amazon QuickSight. Add a widget for each region that contains the aggregated CPU utilization for all EC2 instances that are running in a specific region is incorrect because a data dashboard in AWS QuickSight is primarily used for business intelligence and not to monitor your AWS resources.

The option that says: Enable basic monitoring for all EC2 instances is incorrect because you need to enable detailed monitoring and not just basic monitoring.

questions.question:
A company developed a new website that is ready to be deployed in AWS and be made publicly available over the Internet. But before they do so, the website should support HTTPS since it will be delivering confidential information over the network. The website is required to meet regulatory and compliance requirements for encryption of data in transit.

Which of the following services will enable the company to provision SSL/TLS certificates for its website for encryption of sensitive data in transit and authentication purposes?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS service provisions SSL/TLS certificates to enable HTTPS and encrypt data in transit?
choices: Amazon Artifact
Amazon Route 53
AWS Certificate Manager
AWS IAM
correctAnswers: AWS Certificate Manager
questions.explanation:
AWS Certificate Manager is a service that lets you easily provision, manage, and deploy public and private Secure Sockets Layer/Transport Layer Security (SSL/TLS) certificates for use with AWS services and your internal connected resources. SSL/TLS certificates are used to secure network communications and establish the identity of websites over the Internet as well as resources on private networks.

AWS IAM is incorrect because it is a web service that helps you securely control access to AWS resources. You use IAM to control who is authenticated and authorized to use resources. It does not help you provision SSL/TLS certificates for your website.

Amazon Route 53 is incorrect because it is a highly available and scalable cloud Domain Name System (DNS) web service. You use Route 53 to perform three main functions in any combination: domain registration, DNS routing, and health checking. It does not provide certificates to encrypt your network, unlike Certificate Manager.

Amazon Artifact is incorrect because it is a self-service audit artifact retrieval portal that provides AWS customers with on-demand access to AWS compliance documentation and AWS agreements. You won’t be able to provision SSL/TLS certificates on Amazon Artifact to secure data in transit.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer needs to set up a file-sharing service in AWS with additional features such as data storage and archiving. To implement the architecture, the Administrator will need to use S3 and Glacier. This will be running on EC2 instances with an ELB in front, which will serve as the origin for the CloudFront distribution. To comply with the strict security requirements, the Engineer needs to ensure that data encryption is in place.

Which of the following options will help protect the data at rest and in-transit? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which two AWS solutions protect data at rest in S3/Glacier and data in transit via CloudFront?
choices: Encrypt all data using AWS provided encryption services, and store the encryption keys in an external hardware device.
Enable HTTPS in your CloudFront distribution by using an SSL/TLS certificate provided by AWS Certificate Manager (ACM).
Protect data coming from and to your ELB by applying multiple encryption levels using AWS Encryption SDK.
Encrypt data in S3 and Glacier using AWS provided encryption services, and store the encryption keys in KMS.
Encrypt data in S3 and Glacier using AWS provided encryption services, and store the encryption keys together with the encrypted data.
correctAnswers: Encrypt data in S3 and Glacier using AWS provided encryption services, and store the encryption keys in KMS.
Enable HTTPS in your CloudFront distribution by using an SSL/TLS certificate provided by AWS Certificate Manager (ACM).
questions.explanation:
AWS KMS is a managed service that makes it easy for you to create and control the encryption keys used to encrypt your data and uses FIPS 140-2 validated hardware security modules to protect the security of your keys. It is tightly integrated with S3 and Glacier to provide you with the tools you need to encrypt data at rest.

Similarly, AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) is a service that lets you easily provision, manage, and deploy public and private SSL/TLS certificates for use with AWS services and your internal connected resources. SSL/TLS certificates are used to secure network communications and establish the identity of websites over the Internet as well as resources on private networks. Use it together with your ELB and CloudFront to encrypt data in transit.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Encrypt data in S3 and Glacier using AWS-provided encryption services, and store the encryption keys in KMS.

– Enable HTTPS in your CloudFront distribution by using an SSL/TLS certificate provided by AWS Certificate Manager (ACM).

The option that says: Protect data coming from and to your ELB by applying multiple encryption levels using AWS Encryption SDK is incorrect because there is no need for you to implement your own encryption using the SDK.

The option that says: Encrypt data in S3 and Glacier using AWS provided encryption services, and storing the encryption keys together with the encrypted data is incorrect because you should not store encryption keys together with the data. If one is compromised, then the other is also affected.

The option that says: Encrypt all data using AWS provided encryption services, and storing the encryption keys in an external hardware device is incorrect because the external device might get corrupted or lost, which is problematic. Amazon KMS is a service that can manage your keys more reliably.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer uses AWS CloudFormation to update the resources configuration. After updating the CloudFormation stack, they saw the status UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED. To return the stack to its original state, they must fix the cause of the failure.

Which of the following options would you implement to satisfy the above requirement?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you recover a CloudFormation stack stuck in UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED state?
choices: Use an IAM role and attach all the CloudFormation policies. Select stack actions and choose continue update rollback.
Manually fix the error that causes the update rollback to fail. Select stack actions and choose continue update rollback.
Manually fix the error that causes the update rollback to fail. Select stack actions and choose detect drift.
Use AWS Trusted Advisor to find the recommended actions. Select stack actions and choose detect drift.
correctAnswers: Manually fix the error that causes the update rollback to fail. Select stack actions and choose continue update rollback.
questions.explanation:
AWS CloudFormation is a service that helps you model and set up your Amazon Web Services resources so that you can spend less time managing those resources and more time focusing on your applications that run in AWS. You create a template that describes all the AWS resources that you want (like Amazon EC2 instances or Amazon RDS DB instances), and AWS CloudFormation takes care of provisioning and configuring those resources for you. You don’t need to individually create and configure AWS resources and figure out what’s dependent on what; AWS CloudFormation handles all of that.

Continue rolling back an update - AWS CloudFormation

A stack goes into the UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED state when AWS CloudFormation cannot roll back all changes during an update. To resolve this problem, you must fix the error that causes the update rollback to fail and continue to roll back your stack. If the stack is in a working state or UPDATE_ROLLBACK_COMPLETE state, then you can update the stack again. You can use the AWS CloudFormation console and AWS command-line interface (CLI) to continue rolling back an update.

To continue rolling back an update, you can do the following steps in the CloudFormation console:

– Open the AWS CloudFormation console.

– Select the stack that you want to update, choose Stack actions, and then choose Continue update rollback.

You can also use the advanced option to skip the resources that AWS CloudFormation can’t successfully rollback. You must look up and type the logical IDs of the resources that you want to skip. Specify only resources that went into the UPDATE_FAILED state during the UpdateRollback and not during the forward update.

Hence, the correct answer is: Manually fix the error that causes the update rollback to fail. Select stack actions and choose continue update rollback.

The option that says: Manually fix the error that causes the update rollback to fail. Select stack actions and choose detect drift is incorrect because this won’t help you return the stack to its original state. Drift detection is only used to detect whether a stack’s actual configuration differs from its template configuration. Instead of choosing detect drift, you must select continue update rollback.

The option that says: Use an IAM role and attach all the CloudFormation policies. Select stack actions and choose continue update rollback is incorrect because you need to fix the error first before choosing the update rollback action. Using an IAM Role and CloudFormation policies may not be enough to fix the root cause of this issue.

The option that says: Use AWS Trusted Advisor to find the recommended actions. Select stack actions and choose detect drift is incorrect because Trusted Advisor only inspects your AWS environment and makes recommendations for saving money, improving system performance, or closing security gaps. You must fix the error manually and select continue update rollback to return the stack to its original state.

questions.question:
A web application is hosted on three EC2 instances on different subnets behind an Application Load Balancer with an RDS MySQL database. The CloudOps Engineer configured a custom network ACL and security group for all the EC2 instances and the ALB that explicitly allows incoming traffic on ports 80 and 443. However, the website is still unavailable over the public internet via HTTP and HTTPS.

What should the CloudOps Engineer do to resolve the issue?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Why is a website unreachable over HTTP/HTTPS when inbound ports 80 and 443 are allowed in a custom NACL?
choices: Create a new rule on the custom network ACL to allow outbound traffic on ephemeral ports 1024 – 65535.
Create a new rule on the default network ACL to allow outbound traffic on ports 80 and 443.
Creating a new rule on the default network ACL to allow outbound traffic on ephemeral ports 1024 – 65535.
Create a new rule on the custom network ACL to allow outbound traffic on ports 80 and 443.
correctAnswers: Create a new rule on the custom network ACL to allow outbound traffic on ephemeral ports 1024 – 65535.
questions.explanation:
The following are the basic things that you need to know about network ACLs:

– Your VPC automatically comes with a modifiable default network ACL. By default, it allows all inbound and outbound IPv4 traffic and, if applicable, IPv6 traffic.

– You can create a custom network ACL and associate it with a subnet. By default, each custom network ACL denies all inbound and outbound traffic until you add rules.

– Each subnet in your VPC must be associated with a network ACL. If you don’t explicitly associate a subnet with a network ACL, the subnet is automatically associated with the default network ACL.

– Network ACLs are stateless, which means that responses to allowed inbound traffic are subject to the rules for outbound traffic (and vice versa).

The example network ACL in the preceding section uses an ephemeral port range of 32768-65535. However, you might want to use a different range for your network ACLs depending on the type of client that you’re using or with which you’re communicating.

The client that initiates the request chooses the ephemeral port range. The range varies depending on the client’s operating system.

– Many Linux kernels (including the Amazon Linux kernel) use ports 32768-61000.

– Requests originating from Elastic Load Balancing use ports 1024-65535.

The EC2 instances are already configured with network ACL which means that the subnets where the EC2 instances belong are already configured with custom network ACL.

By default, each custom network ACL denies all inbound and outbound traffic until you add rules. But it is stated in the scenario that inbound traffic on the custom NACL is already configured.

When an EC2 instance in your VPC responds to an HTTP, HTTPS, SSH, or any request, the instance does not automatically respond using the same port number. This means you need to allow outbound traffic on ephemeral ports.

In practice, to cover the different types of clients that might initiate traffic to public-facing instances in your VPC, you can open ephemeral ports 1024-65535.

Hence, the correct answer is: Create a new rule on the custom network ACL to allow outbound traffic on ephemeral ports 1024 – 65535.

The option that says: Create a new rule on the custom network ACL to allow outbound traffic on ports 80 and 443 is incorrect because the instances will not automatically respond on the same port number as the request. You need to use ephemeral ports.

The option that says: Create a new rule on the default network ACL to allow outbound traffic on ports 80 and 443 is incorrect because you don’t need to configure the default ACL since it already allows outbound traffic on all ports. Since there are custom network ACLs configured, you need to explicitly configure ephemeral ports for outbound traffic.

The option that says: Creating a new rule on the default network ACL to allow outbound traffic on ephemeral ports 1024 – 65535 is incorrect because default network ACLs already allow traffic on all ports by default and the issue is still happening. You need to configure the custom network ACLs used by the subnets.

questions.question:
A company launched a global cryptocurrency exchange portal which uses a total of 20 EC2 instances evenly deployed across 4 regions (5 instances per region). An Application Load Balancer has also been set up in each region to distribute the incoming traffic to the EC2 instances.

How can the CloudOps Engineer set up the portal to maintain site availability if one of the 4 regions was to lose network connectivity for an extended period of time?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you configure Route 53 to automatically failover away from an ALB when a region goes down?
choices: 1. Set up a VPN Connection on each of the EC2 instances in each region. 2. Configure a failover using BGP in the case of a region-wide connectivity outage.
1. Set up a Route 53 Latency Based Routing Record Set that resolves to an Application Load Balancer in each region. 2. Set an appropriate health check on each ELB.
1. Set up a Route 53 Latency Based Routing Record Set that resolves to the Application Load Balancers in each region. 2. Set the Evaluate Target Health flag to true.
1. Launch another Application Load Balancer to place in front of the EC2 instances across all regions. 2. Set an appropriate health check on each ELB.
correctAnswers: 1. Set up a Route 53 Latency Based Routing Record Set that resolves to the Application Load Balancers in each region. 2. Set the Evaluate Target Health flag to true.
questions.explanation:
If your application is hosted in multiple AWS Regions, you can improve performance for your users by serving their requests from the AWS Region that provides the lowest latency.

To use latency-based routing, you create latency records for your resources in multiple AWS Regions. When Route 53 receives a DNS query for your domain or subdomain (tutorialsdojo.com or portal.tutorialsdojo.com), it determines which AWS Regions you’ve created latency records for, determines which region gives the user the lowest latency, and then selects a latency record for that region. Route 53 responds with the value from the selected record, such as the IP address for a web server.

Setting an appropriate “Health check on ELB” is not enough. Although this ensures that your ALB is only routing to healthy instances, Route 53 will not know if the ALB in that region has failed.

You need to set the “Evaluate Target Health” flag to true on Route 53. This way, Route 53 will check both ALB entry to ensure that your ALBs are responding. Route 53 will then decide to which ALB it will direct the user. If one region goes down, Route 53 will know it via the “Evaluate Target Health” setting and will not redirect users to that region’s ALB.

Hence, the correct answer is:

1. Set up a Route 53 Latency Based Routing Record Set that resolves to the Application Load Balancers in each region.

2. Set the Evaluate Target Health flag to true.

The option that says: 1. Launch another Application Load Balancer to place in front of the EC2 instances across all regions. 2. Set an appropriate health check on each ELB is incorrect. Although this solution provides redundancy, it does not address the issue of a region-wide connectivity outage. If one of the four regions loses network connectivity, the instances in that region would be unreachable and traffic would still be routed to the non-responsive ALB in that region.

The option that says: 1. Set up a VPN Connection on each of the EC2 instances in each region. 2. Configure a failover using BGP in the case of a region-wide connectivity outage is incorrect because it requires additional infrastructure and configuration. In addition, it does not address the issue of routing traffic to the non-responsive ALB in the disconnected region.

The option that says: 1. Set up a Route 53 Latency Based Routing Record Set that resolves to an Application Load Balancer in each region. 2. Set an appropriate health check on each ELB is incorrect because it also does not address the issue of routing traffic to a non-responsive ALB in a disconnected region.

questions.question:
An application is deployed on us-east1 that runs on Amazon EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling Group called asg-prod. An Application Load Balancer is configured to target the asg-prod when forwarding traffic from Route 53. The CloudOps Engineer needs to configure an Amazon CloudWatch alarm if all instances behind the ALB become unhealthy.

Which condition should be used in CloudWatch?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which CloudWatch metric condition triggers an alarm when all ALB target instances become unhealthy?
choices: Use AWS/EC2 StatusCheckFailed <= 0.
Use AWS/EC2 StatusCheckFailedInstance >= 1.
Use AWS/ApplicationELB HealthyHostCount <= 0.
Use AWS/ApplicationELB UnhealthyHostCount >= 1.
correctAnswers: Use AWS/ApplicationELB HealthyHostCount <= 0.
questions.explanation:
UnHealthyHostCount

The number of targets that are considered unhealthy.

Reporting criteria: Reported if health checks are enabled

Statistics: The most useful statistics are Average, Minimum, and Maximum.

Dimensions

– TargetGroup, LoadBalancer

– TargetGroup, AvailabilityZone, LoadBalancer

HealthyHostCount

The number of targets that are considered healthy.

Reporting criteria: Reported if health checks are enabled

Statistics: The most useful statistics are Average, Minimum, and Maximum.

Dimensions

– TargetGroup, LoadBalancer

– TargetGroup, AvailabilityZone, LoadBalancer

UnhealthyHostCount

If the HealthyHostCount metric has a value of zero, it means that all instances associated with the application load balancer are not healthy.

Hence, the correct answer is: Use AWS/ApplicationELB HealthyHostCount <= 0.

The option that says: Use AWS/ApplicationELB UnhealthyHostCount >= 1 is incorrect because this condition will only trigger once there is an unhealthy host associated with the ALB. This will not check if all instances are unhealthy.

The option that says: Use AWS/EC2 StatusCheckFailedSystem >= 1 is incorrect because this just checks if the underlying hardware of a single EC2 instance is healthy. This does not help you check all the instances associated with the ALB.

The option that says: Use AWS/EC2 StatusCheckFailed <= 0 is incorrect because this just checks if an instance passes both instance and system status checks. You will not be able to check other instances backed by the load balancer.

questions.question:
The development team of a software company has just released the next version of their application in production. It is hosted in an Amazon ECS Cluster with RDS as its database and uses Route 53 as its DNS service. The new version of the application has undergone testing and now needs to be promoted to production from a separate environment. In order to have a smooth transition and avoid any unwanted outages, you need an initial set of traffic to be directed to the new version of the application for testing before doing the final cutover.

Which of the following will you implement on Route 53 to achieve this?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which Route 53 routing policy splits traffic proportionally between old and new application versions?
choices: 1 resource record based on Latency Routing policy
1 resource record based on Geolocation Routing policy
2 resource records based on Simple Routing policy
2 resource records based on Weighted Routing policy
correctAnswers: 2 resource records based on Weighted Routing policy
questions.explanation:
Amazon Route 53 provides highly available and scalable Domain Name System (DNS), domain name registration, and health-checking web services. It is designed to give developers and businesses an extremely reliable and cost effective way to route end users to Internet applications by translating names like example.com into the numeric IP addresses, such as 192.0.2.1, that computers use to connect to each other.

Route 53 weighted routing policy lets you direct traffic to multiple resources in proportions that you specify. It lets you associate multiple resources with a single domain name (tutorialsdojo.com) or a subdomain (portal.tutorialsdojo.com) and choose how much traffic is routed to each resource. This can be useful for a variety of purposes, including load balancing and testing new versions of software.

Hence, the correct answer is: 2 resource records based on Weighted Routing policy.

The option that says: 2 resource records based on Simple Routing policy is incorrect since simple routing policy is used to configure standard DNS records. If you specify multiple values on a simple routing policy, the resolver will return values in a random order.

The option that says: 1 resource record based on Latency Routing policy is incorrect since latency routing is used for resources in multiple AWS regions and you want to route traffic to the region that provides the best latency.

The option that says: 1 resource record based on Geolocation Routing policy is incorrect since geolocation routing is used if you want to route traffic to a specific region based on the location of your users.

questions.question:
A company is using several S3 buckets to store important inventory records of the online supply chain portal. They have an internal management application hosted on a private subnet of the VPC that needs to modify the contents of the S3 bucket and send a report to a partner company via the public Internet. The CloudOps Engineer has created a gateway VPC endpoint for S3 as preparation for this requirement.

Which of the following actions should the Engineer do next?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How should a private subnet's route table be configured to use both an S3 VPC endpoint and a NAT gateway?
choices: Use a NAT gateway to directly send traffic to the VPC S3 endpoint.
Update the private subnet’s route table to directly send all traffic to the public VPC endpoint.
Update the private subnet’s route table to directly connect to the S3 VPC endpoint and send the outbound Internet traffic to a NAT gateway.
Use a NAT gateway to enable the instance to send traffic to the Internet and VPC S3 endpoint.
correctAnswers: Update the private subnet’s route table to directly connect to the S3 VPC endpoint and send the outbound Internet traffic to a NAT gateway.
questions.explanation:
A VPC endpoint enables you to privately connect your VPC to supported AWS services and VPC endpoint services powered by PrivateLink without requiring an Internet gateway, NAT device, VPN connection, or AWS Direct Connect connection. Instances in your VPC do not require public IP addresses to communicate with resources in the service. When your instances send traffic to the Internet (other than the Amazon S3 IP addresses), the traffic is sent to the NAT gateway. Traffic between your VPC and the other service does not leave the Amazon network.

A NAT gateway cannot send traffic over VPC endpoints, AWS Site-to-Site VPN connections, AWS Direct Connect, or VPC peering connections. If your instances in the private subnet must access resources over a VPC endpoint, a Site-to-Site VPN connection, or AWS Direct Connect, use the private subnet’s route table to route the traffic directly to these devices. In this scenario, you can update the private subnet’s route table to directly connect to the S3 VPC endpoint and send the outbound Internet traffic to a NAT gateway.

Hence, updating the private subnet’s route table to directly connect to the S3 VPC endpoint and sending the outbound Internet traffic to a NAT gateway is the correct answer.

Updating the private subnet’s route table to directly send all traffic to the public VPC endpoint is incorrect because a NAT gateway is required to be able to send traffic from your private subnet to the Internet.

Using a NAT gateway to directly send traffic to the VPC S3 endpoint is incorrect. Although you can use a NAT gateway for the Internet connectivity requirement, you still can’t direct your VPC S3 endpoint traffic through the NAT gateway. You have to use the private subnet’s route table to route the traffic directly to your target.

Using a NAT gateway to enable the instance to send traffic to the Internet and VPC S3 endpoint is incorrect because the instance on the private subnet will need a NAT gateway to send traffic to the Internet. However, you can’t use the NAT gateway to send traffic to the VPC endpoint without doing any configuration to the route table of the private subnet.

questions.question:
A leading insurance firm has a customer portal that is hosted on a fleet of Spot EC2 instances which are part of an Auto Scaling Group. The application is being used quite extensively during office hours from 8 in the morning till 4 in the afternoon. Users are complaining that the performance of the application is slow during the start of the day after which the application works properly.

Which of the following can be done to ensure that the application works properly at the beginning of the day?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which Auto Scaling policy ensures instances are ready before predictable daily peak traffic starts?
choices: Add a Dynamic scaling policy for the Auto Scaling group to launch new instances based on the Memory utilization.
Add a Scheduled scaling policy for the Auto Scaling group to launch new instances before the start of the day.
Add a Dynamic scaling policy for the Auto Scaling group to launch new instances based on the CPU utilization.
Add an Application Load Balancer (ALB) to the entire setup to ensure that the traffic is properly distributed on the instances.
correctAnswers: Add a Scheduled scaling policy for the Auto Scaling group to launch new instances before the start of the day.
questions.explanation:
Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling helps you ensure that you have the correct number of Amazon EC2 instances available to handle the load for your application. You create collections of EC2 instances, called Auto Scaling groups. You can specify the minimum number of instances in each Auto Scaling group, and Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling ensures that your group never goes below this size. You can specify the maximum number of instances in each Auto Scaling group, and Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling ensures that your group never goes above this size.

In this scenario, you will need to configure a Scheduled scaling policy. This will ensure that the instances are already scaled up and ready before the start of the day since this is when the application is used the most.

Hence, the correct answer is: Add a Scheduled scaling policy for the Auto Scaling group to launch new instances before the start of the day.

The options that say: Add a Dynamic scaling policy for the Auto Scaling group to launch new instances based on the CPU utilization and Add a Dynamic scaling policy for the Auto Scaling group to launch new instances based on the Memory utilization are incorrect. Although this is a valid solution, it is still better to configure a Scheduled scaling policy as you already know the exact peak hours of your application. By the time either the CPU or Memory hits a peak, the application already has performance issues, so you need to ensure the scaling is done beforehand using a Scheduled scaling policy.

The option that says: Add an Application Load Balancer (ALB) to the entire setup to ensure that the traffic is properly distributed on the instances is incorrect. Although the Application load balancer can also balance the traffic, it cannot increase the instances based on demand.

questions.question:
A company has multiple AWS accounts that are consolidated using AWS Organizations. A CloudOps Engineer has been tasked to set up AWS IAM Identity Center to centrally manage access to all of the company’s AWS accounts and cloud applications. The Engineer has already created a directory in the master account using the AWS Directory Service. Full access has also been configured by the Engineer in Organizations.

Which of the following should the Engineer configure to complete the setup?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What must be configured in IAM Identity Center to grant users access to AWS accounts?
choices: Set up permission sets in IAM Identity Center to give AWS access to IAM Identity Center Users.
Set up permission sets in Organizations. Associate the permission sets with IAM Identity Center users and groups.
Set up service control policies (SCPs) in Organizations. Associate the SCPs with Directory Service users and groups using the AWS Management Console.
For each member account, set up IAM roles that will be used by IAM Identity Center. Associate the users with these IAM roles using IAM Identity Center.
correctAnswers: Set up permission sets in IAM Identity Center to give AWS access to IAM Identity Center Users.
questions.explanation:
AWS AWS IAM Identity Center manages access to all your AWS Organizations accounts and other business applications that support the Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML) 2.0 standard.

You can configure your identity source in AWS IAM Identity Center to determine where your users and groups are stored. Once configured, you can then look up users or groups in your store to grant them single sign-on access to AWS accounts, cloud applications, or both.

AWS IAM Identity Center

AWS IAM Identity Center automatically provides you with a store by default, which you can use to manage your users and groups within AWS IAM Identity Center. If you choose to store them in AWS IAM Identity Center, create your users and groups and assign their level of access to your AWS accounts and applications. Alternatively, you can choose to Connect to Your External Identity Provider using Microsoft Entra ID or connect to your Microsoft AD Directory using AWS Directory Service.

A permission set is a collection of administrator-defined policies that AWS IAM Identity Center uses to determine a user’s effective permissions to access a given AWS account. Permission sets can contain either AWS-managed policies or custom policies that are stored in the AWS IAM Identity Center. Policies are essentially documents that act as containers for one or more permission statements. These statements represent individual access controls (allow or deny) for various tasks that determine what tasks users can or cannot perform within the AWS account.

Permission sets

Permission sets are stored in the AWS IAM Identity Center and are only used for AWS accounts. They are not used to manage access to cloud applications. Permission sets ultimately get created as IAM roles in a given AWS account, with trust policies that allow users to assume the role through the AWS IAM Identity Center.

Hence, the correct answer is: Set up permission sets in IAM Identity Center to give AWS access to IAM Identity Center Users.

The option that says: For each member account, set up IAM roles that will be used by IAM Identity Center. Associate the users with these IAM roles using IAM Identity Center is incorrect. In the AWS Identity Center, the level of access that users can have in an account is defined using permission sets. You cannot associate Identity Center users directly with IAM roles.

The option that says: Set up permission sets in Organizations. Associate the permission sets with IAM Identity Center users and groups is incorrect because you can only set up permission sets in AWS IAM Identity Center and not in AWS Organizations.

The option that says: Set up service control policies (SCPs) in Organizations. Associate the SCPs with Directory Service users and groups using the AWS Management Console is incorrect. Service control policies (SCPs) is a feature of AWS Organizations primarily designed to set the maximum permissions that identities (users and roles) in member accounts can have. They cannot be directly associated with Directory Service users.

questions.question:
A company would like to hire a new Administrators who will manage the backups of their AWS resources. However, doing so will increase the company’s operating expenses, and thus, they are looking for ways to automate their processes to cut costs. The company is using point-in-time snapshots of its resources provisioned on AWS.

Which of the following services provides you with a user-configurable automatic backup service and storage scaling without the need for regular user intervention? (Select TWO)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which two AWS services provide automatic backup and storage scaling without manual intervention?
choices: AWS DataSync
Amazon RDS
Amazon S3
Amazon EBS
Amazon Aurora
correctAnswers: Amazon RDS
Amazon Aurora
questions.explanation:
Amazon RDS and Amazon Aurora are the correct answers.

AWS lessens the burden of maintaining your backups by creating the backups for you.

Amazon RDS creates and saves automated backups of your DB instance during the backup window of your DB instance. RDS creates a storage volume snapshot of your DB instance, backing up the entire DB instance and not just individual databases. RDS saves the automated backups of your DB instance according to the backup retention period that you specify. By default, an RDS created from the AWS console has backup retention of 7 days. You can further modify this backup retention period between 0-35 days.

For Amazon Aurora, it backs up your cluster volume automatically and retains restore data for the length of the backup retention period. Aurora backups are continuous and incremental, so you can quickly restore to any point within the backup retention period. No performance impact or interruption of database service occurs as backup data is being written. You can specify a backup retention period, from 1 to 35 days, when you create or modify a DB cluster.

The option: Amazon S3 is incorrect because S3 has a lifecycle policy but not a backup policy. Standard S3 is already very durable, and AWS has no options for automatic backup on S3.

The option: Amazon EBS is incorrect. Although EBS volumes can be automatically backed up using AWS Backup, they don’t support auto-scaling storage-wise.

The option: AWS DataSync is incorrect because this service is just a data transfer tool.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer is creating a secure VPC for a webchat application. The manager wants to make sure that the backend is not accessible to the public. Web servers will be placed in a public subnet, while database servers will be placed in a private subnet. The database servers should still be able to fetch the critical software patches and updates from the public Internet. It is expected that many connections will be made by the webchat application, so the Engineer has to allocate enough IP addresses available for use.

What are the steps in designing such a VPC?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What are the correct steps to design a VPC with public and private subnets using a NAT Gateway?
choices: 1. Launch VPC Wizard and choose VPC with Public and Private Subnet including a NAT Gateway 2. Allocate an IPv6 CIDR block of 10.0.0.0/16 3. Create a new Elastic IP address and attach it to your NAT gateway 4. Set up the necessary security groups for the public and private subnets including a Network ACL for the NAT gateway

1. Launch VPC Wizard and Choose VPC with Public and Private Subnet including an egress-only Internet gateway 2. Allocate an IPv4 CIDR block of 10.0.0.0/16 3. Create a new Elastic IP address and attach it to your egress-only Internet gateway 4. Set up the necessary security groups for the public and private subnets

1. Launch VPC Wizard and Choose VPC with Public and Private Subnet including a NAT Instance 2. Allocate an IPv6 CIDR block of 10.0.0.0/16 3. Create a new Elastic IP address and attach it to your NAT Instance 4. Set up the necessary security groups for the public and private subnets as well as for the NAT Instance (NATSG)

1. Launch VPC Wizard and choose VPC with Public and Private Subnet including a NAT Gateway 2. Allocate an IPv4 CIDR block of 10.0.0.0/16 3. Create a new Elastic IP address and attach it to your NAT gateway 4. Set up the necessary security groups for the public and private subnets including a Network ACL for the NAT gateway
correctAnswers: 1. Launch VPC Wizard and choose VPC with Public and Private Subnet including a NAT Gateway 2. Allocate an IPv4 CIDR block of 10.0.0.0/16 3. Create a new Elastic IP address and attach it to your NAT gateway 4. Set up the necessary security groups for the public and private subnets including a Network ACL for the NAT gateway
questions.explanation:
An Elastic IP address is a static IPv4 address designed for dynamic cloud computing. An Elastic IP address is allocated to your AWS account, and is yours until you release it. By using an Elastic IP address, you can mask the failure of an instance or software by rapidly remapping the address to another instance in your account. Alternatively, you can specify the Elastic IP address in a DNS record for your domain, so that your domain points to your instance.

For this type of VPC configuration, you first need to create an Elastic IP address for your NAT gateway, which will allow your private subnet to connect to the outside Internet. After launching the wizard, allocate an IPv4 CIDR block of value 10.0.0.0/16, which will supplement you with the IP range needed. Then attach your allocated elastic IP to finish creating your VPC and exit the wizard.

Hence, the correct answer is:

1. Launch VPC Wizard and choose VPC with Public and Private Subnet including a NAT Gateway

2. Allocate an IPv4 CIDR block of 10.0.0.0/16

3. Create a new Elastic IP address and attach it to your NAT gateway

4. Set up the necessary security groups for the public and private subnets including a Network ACL for the NAT gateway

The following options:

1. Launch VPC Wizard and Choose VPC with Public and Private Subnet including a NAT Instance

2. Allocate an IPv6 CIDR block of 10.0.0.0/16

3. Create a new Elastic IP address and attach it to your NAT Instance

4. Set up the necessary security groups for the public and private subnets as well as for the NAT Instance (NATSG)

and

1. Launch VPC Wizard and choose VPC with Public and Private Subnet including a NAT Gateway

2. Allocate an IPv6 CIDR block of 10.0.0.0/16

3. Create a new Elastic IP address and attach it to your NAT gateway

4. Set up the necessary security groups for the public and private subnets including a Network ACL for the NAT gateway

are both incorrect because of various factors. First, you can only use an egress-only Internet gateway for VPC with IPv6 and not NAT Instance/Gateway. Second, You cannot explicitly specify an IP range for IPv6, as this is only provided by Amazon.

The following option:

1. Launch VPC Wizard and Choose VPC with Public and Private Subnet including an egress-only Internet gateway

2. Allocate an IPv4 CIDR block of 10.0.0.0/16

3. Create a new Elastic IP address and attach it to your egress-only Internet gateway

4. Set up the necessary security groups for the public and private subnets

is incorrect because, for IPv4, you cannot use an egress-only Internet gateway.

questions.question:
A photo-sharing company is hosting an application on an Amazon EC2 instance that allows users to upload images. The application saves the images on an Amazon S3 bucket in us-east-2. The company has also enabled AWS-ConfigureS3BucketLogging to track upload activity and monitor access patterns. Recently, users have been experiencing load-time issues when uploading images to the application. The website is popular in countries away from us-east-2.

Which of the following should the CloudOps Engineer do to address the issue?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you improve S3 upload performance for users geographically distant from the bucket's region?
choices: Create accelerators and listeners using AWS Global Accelerator.
Build S3 access points in AWS Regions closest to the users.
Use the S3 CreateMultipartUpload API to improve upload performance for large images.
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket.
correctAnswers: Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket.
questions.explanation:
Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration is a bucket-level feature that enables fast, easy, and secure transfers of files over long distances between your client and an S3 bucket. Transfer Acceleration takes advantage of the globally distributed edge locations in Amazon CloudFront. As the data arrives at an edge location, the data is routed to Amazon S3 over an optimized network path.

Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration

Hence, the correct answer is: Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket.

The option that says: Create accelerators and listeners using AWS Global Accelerator is incorrect because the Global Accelerator service does not work with S3. It only supports endpoints like application load balancers, network load balancers, EC2 instances, or elastic IP addresses.

The option that says: Build S3 access points in AWS Regions closest to the users is incorrect because S3 access points are only used for creating access policies that grant permissions to groups of users or applications on S3 buckets. It is not primarily used to improve upload speed on a bucket.

The option that says: Use the S3 CreateMultipartUpload API to improve upload performance for large images is incorrect because while the multipart upload API allows for efficient parallel transfers of large files, it does not reduce latency caused by geographic distance from the S3 bucket. Transfer Acceleration is necessary to optimize global upload performance by utilizing edge locations.

questions.question:
A Cloud Ops Engineer plans to set up a disaster and recovery plan in AWS. The requirement is to establish a durable, highly available backup and archiving strategy for the company-owned financial documents, which should be accessible immediately for 6 months. It is expected that there would be a compliance audit every 3 years. The Engineer needs to ensure that the files are still available during that period.

Which service should the Engineer use to fulfill these requirements in the most cost-effective manner?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What cost-effective AWS solution stores documents for immediate access then archives them long-term?
choices: Upload data to an Amazon S3 bucket. Use lifecycle policies to move the data to Amazon Glacier for archiving.
Set up a Direct Connect connection to upload data to an Amazon S3 bucket. For archiving purposes, use IAM policies to move the data into Amazon Glacier.
Set up a storage gateway to upload data in an Amazon S3 bucket. Configure lifecycle policies to archive the data to a Cold HDD Amazon EBS volume.
Use AWS DataSync to transfer the data to an Amazon S3 bucket for storage. Schedule periodic transfers to ensure backups are synchronized for compliance audits.
correctAnswers: Upload data to an Amazon S3 bucket. Use lifecycle policies to move the data to Amazon Glacier for archiving.
questions.explanation:
To manage your objects so that they are stored cost-effectively throughout their lifecycle, configure their Amazon S3 Lifecycle. An S3 Lifecycle configuration is a set of rules that define actions that Amazon S3 applies to a group of objects.

You can use lifecycle policies to define actions that you want Amazon S3 to take during an object’s lifetime (for example, transition objects to another storage class, archive them, or delete them after a specified period of time).

Amazon S3 Lifecycle

You can add rules in a lifecycle configuration to tell Amazon S3 to transition objects to another Amazon S3 storage class. For example:

– When you know objects are infrequently accessed, you might transition them to the STANDARD_IA storage class.

– You might want to archive objects that you don’t need to access in real-time to the GLACIER storage class.

Hence, the correct answer is: Upload data to an Amazon S3 bucket. Use lifecycle policies to move the data to Amazon Glacier for archiving.

The option that says: Set up a storage gateway to upload data in an Amazon S3 bucket. Configure lifecycle policies to archive the data to a Cold HDD Amazon EBS volume is incorrect. It’s not possible to transition data from Amazon S3 to Amazon EBS via lifecycle policies.

The option that says: Set up a Direct Connect connection to upload data to an Amazon S3 bucket. For archiving purposes, use IAM policies to move the data into Amazon Glacier is incorrect. Direct Connect is not required to transfer data to Amazon S3 unless a private connection between an on-premises data center and AWS is specified. Also, IAM policies only set the permissions needed to carry out an action in AWS. S3 lifecycle policy is the correct answer.

The option that says: Use AWS DataSync to transfer the data to an Amazon S3 bucket for storage. Schedule periodic transfers to ensure backups are synchronized for compliance audits is incorrect because DataSync is primarily used for large-scale data migration and synchronization rather than archival management. It does not automate lifecycle transitions to archival storage like Glacier. It is not ideal for a long-term and cost-effective solution.

questions.question:
A RegTech startup plans to utilize machine learning to improve its financial regulatory processes. They have a fleet of Spot EC2 instances with an Application Load Balancer to host their online customer portal. There is a requirement to produce a report that provides a list of IP addresses that are accessing their portal, including the API request logs that went through all of their AWS resources.

Which of the following options could help the CloudOps Engineer achieve this requirement? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which two AWS services capture incoming IP addresses and API request logs across all resources?
choices: Amazon VPC Flow Logs and AWS CloudTrail
AWS ELB Access Logs and AWS CloudTrail
AWS CloudTrail and AWS Config
AWS ELB Access Logs and AWS Config
Amazon CloudWatch and AWS CloudTrail
correctAnswers: Amazon VPC Flow Logs and AWS CloudTrail
AWS ELB Access Logs and AWS CloudTrail
questions.explanation:
In this scenario, you could use VPC Flow Logs or ELB Access Logs to get the list of IP addresses that are accessing their portal. Use CloudTrail to log all API requests made to your AWS resources.

AWS CloudTrail is an AWS service that helps you enable governance, compliance, and operational and risk auditing of your AWS account. Actions taken by a user, role, or AWS service are recorded as events in CloudTrail. Events include actions taken in the AWS Management Console, AWS Command Line Interface, and AWS SDKs and APIs.

VPC Flow Logs is a feature that enables you to capture information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in your VPC. Flow log data can be published to Amazon CloudWatch Logs and Amazon S3. After you’ve created a flow log, you can retrieve and view its data in the chosen destination.

Flow logs can help you with a number of tasks, for example, to troubleshoot why specific traffic is not reaching an instance, which in turn helps you diagnose overly restrictive security group rules. You can also use flow logs as a security tool to monitor the traffic that is reaching your instance.

Elastic Load Balancing (ELB) provides access logs that capture detailed information about requests sent to your load balancer. Each log contains information such as the time the request was received, the client’s IP address, latencies, request paths, and server responses. You can use these access logs to analyze traffic patterns and troubleshoot issues.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Amazon VPC Flow Logs and AWS CloudTrail

– AWS ELB Access Logs and AWS CloudTrail

AWS ELB Access Logs and AWS Config are incorrect. Even though you can use ELB Access Logs to capture the IP addresses, AWS Config will not be able to provide you with the API logging you need to meet the requirement.

Amazon CloudWatch and AWS CloudTrail are incorrect. Even though you can use CloudTrail to get the logs for the API requests of your AWS resources, CloudWatch will not be able to provide you with the specific IP addresses that come into your VPC.

AWS CloudTrail and AWS Config are incorrect because AWS Config is simply a service used for configuration management and not for tracking incoming traffic to your VPC.

questions.question:
A leading media company is utilizing a wide range of instance store-backed and EBS-backed EC2 instances in their VPC. There are a couple of instances that always go from the pending state to the terminated state immediately after restarting it.

Which of the following is a possible cause of this problem? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What are two reasons an EC2 instance immediately transitions from pending to terminated state?
choices: You have reached the limit on the number of instances that you can launch in a region.
AWS does not currently have enough available On-Demand capacity to service your request.
You've reached your EBS volume limit.
The instance store-backed AMI that you used to launch the instance is missing a required part.
Each instance has an Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) attached.
correctAnswers: You've reached your EBS volume limit.
The instance store-backed AMI that you used to launch the instance is missing a required part.
questions.explanation:
If your EC2 instance goes from the pending state to the terminated state immediately after restarting then it could be caused by one of the following reasons:

You’ve reached your EBS volume limit. An EBS snapshot is corrupt. The root EBS volume is encrypted and you do not have permissions to access the KMS key for decryption. The instance store-backed AMI that you used to launch the instance is missing a required part (an image.part.xx file).

Hence, the correct answers are:

– You’ve reached your EBS volume limit.

– The instance store-backed AMI that you used to launch the instance is missing a required part.

The option that says: Each instance has an Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) attached is incorrect because an Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) is simply a network interface for Amazon EC2 instances that enables customers to run applications requiring high levels of inter-node communications at scale on AWS. Having an EFA attached to the instance won’t result in this error.

The option that says: You have reached the limit on the number of instances that you can launch in a region is incorrect because this option describes the root cause for getting an InstanceLimitExceeded error.
